title: Policy, Market Failure & Ethiopian Reforms
description: Market failures, fiscal and monetary policy, taxation, and Ethiopia's macroeconomic reforms

questions.statement: Public goods are non-excludable and non-rival in consumption.
questions.payload: TRUE. National defence and street lighting are examples because no one can be excluded and use by one does not reduce availability.

questions.statement: The free market tends to overproduce public goods because they are profitable.
questions.payload: FALSE. The free market underprovides public goods due to the free-rider problem.

questions.statement: A negative externality such as factory pollution leads to overproduction by the free market.
questions.payload: TRUE. Producers ignore social costs, so output exceeds the socially optimal level.

questions.statement: Education creates a positive externality, so the free market tends to underproduce it.
questions.payload: TRUE. The social benefit of education exceeds the private benefit, leading to underproduction.

questions.statement: A monopoly restricts output and raises price above the competitive level, creating inefficiency.
questions.payload: TRUE. Monopoly power causes deadweight loss by producing less than the socially optimal quantity.

questions.statement: Fiscal policy refers to the central bank's control of money supply and interest rates.
questions.payload: FALSE. That is monetary policy; fiscal policy uses government taxation and spending.

questions.statement: Expansionary fiscal policy involves cutting taxes and increasing government spending.
questions.payload: TRUE. This stimulates aggregate demand and is used during recession.

questions.statement: Contractionary fiscal policy is used to reduce unemployment during a recession.
questions.payload: FALSE. Contractionary policy controls inflation; expansionary policy reduces unemployment.

questions.statement: Monetary policy in Ethiopia is controlled by the National Bank of Ethiopia.
questions.payload: TRUE. The NBE sets interest rates, controls money supply, and regulates commercial banks.

questions.statement: Open market operations involve the government directly setting prices for all goods.
questions.payload: FALSE. OMOs involve the central bank buying and selling government securities to adjust money supply.

questions.statement: A progressive tax takes a higher proportion of income from higher earners.
questions.payload: TRUE. Progressive taxes like personal income tax reduce income inequality.

questions.statement: VAT is a direct tax paid by the person on whom it is originally levied.
questions.payload: FALSE. VAT is an indirect tax shifted to consumers through higher prices.

questions.statement: VAT tends to be regressive because it takes a larger share of income from poorer households.
questions.payload: TRUE. Poorer households spend a higher proportion of their income on goods subject to VAT.

questions.statement: A regressive tax takes a lower effective rate from higher earners.
questions.payload: TRUE. Regressive taxes worsen inequality because the poor pay a higher share of their income.

questions.statement: Adam Smith's canons of taxation include equity, certainty, convenience, and economy.
questions.payload: TRUE. These four principles ensure taxes are fair, predictable, easy to pay, and cheap to collect.

questions.statement: Ethiopia had a free market economy under the Derg regime from 1974 to 1991.
questions.payload: FALSE. The Derg ran a command or socialist economy with state ownership and central planning.

questions.statement: Ethiopia's transition to a market-oriented economy began after 1991 under the EPRDF.
questions.payload: TRUE. Post-1991 reforms included liberalisation, privatisation, and allowing private investment.

questions.statement: Under the Derg, land and major firms were nationalised and foreign markets were largely closed.
questions.payload: TRUE. The Derg pursued state control, nationalisation, and isolation from global markets.

questions.statement: Information asymmetry occurs when all parties in a transaction have equal information.
questions.payload: FALSE. Information asymmetry means one party has more information than the other.

questions.statement: Merit goods like healthcare and education are goods that society wants more of than the free market provides.
questions.payload: TRUE. The free market underprovides merit goods because their social benefits exceed private benefits.
